series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/4] mm: Move demotion related functions in memory-tiers.c
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves functions related to demoting memory usage in the Linux kernel from mm/vmscan.c to mm/memory-tiers.c, as per Alexandre Ghiti's suggestion. The goal is to centralize these functions in a single file for better organization and maintainability. No functional changes are intended by this move.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/4] mm: Rename node_get_allowed_targets() to make it more explicit
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the function `node_get_allowed_targets()` to `node_get_allowed_demotion_targets()` in the Linux kernel's memory management code. The original function name was misleading, as it actually returns tier nodes targeted during demotion, not allowed targets. This change aims to make the function name more explicit and accurate. No functional changes are intended by this patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/4] mm: Fix demotion gfp by clearing GFP_RECLAIM after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a demotion issue in the Linux kernel's memory management system by clearing GFP_RECLAIM after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE. The problem was caused by an incorrect ordering of these flags, which led to inconsistent behavior between regular and transparent huge page (THP) allocations. The fix involves simply swapping the order of operations, ensuring that GFP_RECLAIM is cleared before GFP_TRANSHUGE is set.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton requested separation of stable fixes from cleanups and preparation against current mainline, as well as careful changelog description of userspace-visible runtime effects.
review_comments.raw_body:
Please let's have the cc:stable fixes separated out from the cleanups,
and prepared against current -linus mainline.

Also, when proposing backportable fixes please ensure that the
changelogs carefully describe the userspace-visible runtime effects of
the bug.

Thanks.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Johannes Weiner noted that the original patch's fix inadvertently disabled direct reclaim for THP allocations, making the request unnecessarily wimpy, and suggested a clearer comment to explain GFP_TRANSHUGE's own reclaim policy.
review_comments.raw_body:
I don't think this is right.

The Fixes: did it this way to disable kswapd for THP allocations,
while still allowing the customary direct reclaim. Maybe a better
comment would have been: /* GFP_TRANSHUGE has its own reclaim policy */

After your fix, direct reclaim isn't allowed either, which makes the
request unnecessarily wimpy.

The Closes: refers to reclaim that should be avoided during demotion.
But if this path is taken during demotion it will already not recurse
into direct reclaim due to PF_MEMALLOC.

So I don't see a bug in the existing code. But maybe the comment could
be clearer.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David requested that fixes be moved to the top of the patchset or sent independently
review_comments.raw_body:
Also, please move fixes to the very beginning of the patch set, or
better, send them independently.

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Gregory Price noted that the GFP_RECLAIM flag is being masked and added back in multiple layers, making it difficult to reason about what the actual flags are at any given point.
review_comments.raw_body:
The bigger issue how many times we see this particular flag getting
masked and apparently added back in at multiple layers. We saw two
or three paths (some unreachable) that can twiddle RECLAIM flags in
the stack for demotion (which is in reclaim already, so do the flags
matter?).

It makes it difficult to reason about what the GFP flags actually
are at any given point.

But yeah I wasn't sure to make of this code, it could be as you
suggested just a bad comment.

~Gregory

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/4] mm: Fix demotion gfp by preserving initial gfp reclaim policy
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a demotion gfp issue in the Linux kernel's memory management subsystem, specifically when handling huge pages and migration targets. The problem arises from the unconditioned enabling of reclaim during demotion, which can lead to premature folio refcount drops. To fix this, the initial GFP flags are preserved by modifying the alloc_mask to exclude direct reclaim.

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the btrfs_log_dev_io_error() function to log device I/O errors for all bio error status codes, not just BLK_STS_IOERR and BLK_STS_TARGET. This change aims to prevent incorrect conclusions about system behavior when encountering different error codes from the block layer.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with a Reviewed-by tag, indicating it meets kernel coding standards.
review_comments.raw_body:
\u5728 2026/3/14 05:56, Boris Burkov \u5199\u9053:
> As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> 
> For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().
> 
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>

Reviewed-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>

Thanks,
Qu


> ---
>   fs/btrfs/bio.c | 3 +--
>   1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 2 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/bio.c b/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> index 2a2a21aec817..08b1d62603d0 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> @@ -352,8 +352,7 @@ static void btrfs_log_dev_io_error(const struct bio *bio, struct btrfs_device *d
>   {
>   	if (!dev || !dev->bdev)
>   		return;
> -	if (bio->bi_status != BLK_STS_IOERR && bio->bi_status != BLK_STS_TARGET)
> -		return;
> +	ASSERT(bio->bi_status);
>   
>   	if (btrfs_op(bio) == BTRFS_MAP_WRITE)
>   		btrfs_dev_stat_inc_and_print(dev, BTRFS_DEV_STAT_WRITE_ERRS);

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: reviewer noted that the patch introduces a small overhead in terms of text size and cache footprint, questioning whether this added complexity is justified by the benefits of improved maintainability
review_comments.raw_body:
guard() is cute, but this patch adds a little overhead - defconfig
page_alloc.o text increases by 32 bytes, presumably all in
reserve_highatomic_pageblock().  More instructions, larger cache
footprint.

So we're adding a little overhead to every user's Linux machine for all
time.  In return for which the developers get a little convenience and
maintainability.

Is it worth it?

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that zone lock guards are not properly ordered with other locks in the mm functions, specifically mentioning that __offline_isolated_pages() holds the zone lock while also holding the page lock, and requested a reordering of the locks to fix this issue

reviewer questioned whether the introduction of zone lock guards would result in a measurable performance slowdown, and requested data on this aspect

reviewer suggested that the zone lock guard approach be reconsidered in favor of improving the generic locking infrastructure, potentially for performance reasons

Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2.

Author acknowledged that the unconditional usage of zone lock guards could introduce overhead in all kernel builds, agreed to explore improvements to the guard infrastructure to reduce or eliminate this overhead.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for taking a look and digging into this.

[...]

---

That's a fair point, but as I understand Andrew's main concern, the
guard() usage becomes part of the code unconditionally, with no way to
disable it, whereas tracepoints can be compiled out. Any overhead
introduced by guards is therefore carried by all kernel builds.

Given that, improvements to the guard infrastructure itself seem worth
exploring regardless of whether this particular patchset ends up going
in. If the overhead can be reduced or eliminated in the common case, it
should make the trade-off much easier.

Thanks again for investigating this and suggesting a possible approach.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 1/8] mm: use zone lock guard in reserve_highatomic_pageblock()

version_history.subject: [PATCH 1/8] mm: use zone lock guard in reserve_highatomic_pageblock()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch updates the reserve_highatomic_pageblock() function in the Linux kernel's page allocation code to use a zone lock guard instead of explicit lock/unlock and goto out_unlock patterns. The zone lock guard is a new feature introduced by this patch, which provides automatic scope-based cleanup when exiting the locked region. This change simplifies the code and reduces the risk of errors due to forgotten unlocks or incorrect locking orders. The patch also updates the include/linux/mmzone_lock.h file to define the zone lock guard and its attributes.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2, but did not provide a clear plan for addressing Peter's concern about the lock ordering.

Author acknowledged that the use of zone lock guard could introduce overhead in all kernel builds, but suggests exploring improvements to the guard infrastructure to mitigate this issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for taking a look and digging into this.

[...]

---

That's a fair point, but as I understand Andrew's main concern, the
guard() usage becomes part of the code unconditionally, with no way to
disable it, whereas tracepoints can be compiled out. Any overhead
introduced by guards is therefore carried by all kernel builds.

Given that, improvements to the guard infrastructure itself seem worth
exploring regardless of whether this particular patchset ends up going
in. If the overhead can be reduced or eliminated in the common case, it
should make the trade-off much easier.

Thanks again for investigating this and suggesting a possible approach.

review_comments.author: Dan Williams
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Dan Williams noted that the zone_lock_irqsave lock guard does not properly handle the case where reserve_highatomic_pageblock() is called from an interrupt context, and suggested using a different locking mechanism to avoid potential deadlocks.

Reviewer Dan Williams suggested using __GUARD_IS_ERR() only when a better implementation is available, and confirmed the patch works fine in the CXL subsystem tests.
review_comments.raw_body:
Peter Zijlstra wrote:
[..]

---

I think it makes sense, do not make everyone pay the cost of
__GUARD_IS_ERR() at least until a better __GUARD_IS_ERR() comes along.

I gave the below a run through the CXL subsystem tests which uses
conditional guards quite a bit. Worked fine, and looks good to me.

So feel free to add a "tested by" from me. Not putting the actual tag
here so that b4 does not slurp a tag for the wrong patchset.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/2] EVM: add comment describing why ino field is still unsigned long
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds a comment to the EVM code explaining why the inode number field is still an unsigned long, despite the inode->i_ino being widened to u64. The reason is that existing HMAC and signatures from 32-bit hosts should continue working when i_ino hasn't changed and fits in a u32.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/2] nfs: delegated attribute fixes

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/2] nfs: fix utimensat() for atime with delegated timestamps
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS client where delegated timestamps are not properly updated when utimensat() is called for atime only. The fix involves changing the client to issue an RPC to update the ctime alongside the atime.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/2] nfs: update inode ctime after removexattr operation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the inode ctime after a removexattr operation in NFS to ensure that the client's cached timestamp is refreshed. The change follows the same strategy as setxattr, which includes a trailing GETATTR to update the ctime. This fix addresses an issue where xfstest generic/728 fails with delegated timestamps.

series_items.patch_summary: The patch removes NULL checks in callers of lock_list_lru_of_memcg() when adding or deleting items from a list_lru, as acquiring the lock will not fail due to cgroup hierarchy walking.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/7] mm: list_lru: deduplicate unlock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: The patch consolidates the unlock_list_lru() function into a single implementation, removing duplicate code in the MEMCG and !MEMCG variants.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/7] mm: list_lru: move list dead check to lock_list_lru_of_memcg()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the check for list dead condition from lock_list_lru() to its caller, allowing for unification of the lock_list_lru() variants in a subsequent patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 4/7] mm: list_lru: deduplicate lock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the lock_list_lru() function by sharing its implementation between MEMCG and non-MEMCG paths, reducing code duplication.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 5/7] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API, allowing callers to keep auxiliary state synchronized with LRU state. The THP shrinker is an example use case that requires this functionality.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the locking primitives and their interaction with existing code. Suggested a more detailed explanation of the locking mechanism and its implications for concurrent access.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 6/7] mm: list_lru: introduce memcg_list_lru_alloc_folio()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a helper function `memcg_list_lru_alloc_folio()` to allocate list_lru heads for folio objects, which is necessary for switching the THP shrinker to list_lru. The function uses RCU protection and pinning of the cgroup only when needed, making the existence check lightweight under RCU.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the implementation of `memcg_list_lru_alloc_folio()` and suggested an alternative approach using a different data structure.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 7/7] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses an issue in the deferred split shrinker, which handles cgroups suboptimally by using a per-NUMA node or per-cgroup queue instead of the intersection. The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail, causing large pages to be split prematurely. The patch converts the deferred split queue to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists and streamlines list operations.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to switch the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker from its current implementation to use list_lru instead. The change involves refactoring and cleaning up existing code, extending the list_lru API to accommodate private state synchronization, and finally switching the deferred_split_queue to list_lru.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 00/11] mm/zswap, zsmalloc: Per-memcg-lruvec zswap accounting
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces per-memcg-lruvec zswap accounting by moving memory-cgroup awareness to the zsmalloc layer, allowing for more accurate tracking of compressed memory usage and enabling cgroups to control where their zswapped objects live. The changes also enable future consumers to account memory statistics using a new compressed object memory accounting infrastructure. The patch series includes 11 patches that make various modifications to the zswap and zsmalloc code to achieve this goal, including introducing a new per-zspage array of objcg pointers to track per-memcg-lruvec memory usage. The changes are expected to improve performance by reducing remote access latency costs and enabling more effective reclaim when pressure is localized to a single cgroup.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about inconsistent naming conventions in the zsmalloc code by renaming zs_object_copy to zs_obj_copy, and also fixed an issue where find_alloced_obj was returning a signed int instead of an unsigned int.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that when pool->memcg_aware is not set, writing to zspage->objcgs[obj_idx] will access uninitialized memory and likely crash; he suggested adding a conditional statement to avoid the write if the WARN_ON_ONCE warning fires.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Mar 11, 2026 at 12:52 PM Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> All the zsmalloc functions that operate on a zsmalloc object (encoded
> location values) are named "zs_obj_xxx", except for zs_object_copy.
>
> Rename zs_object_copy to zs_obj_copy to conform to the pattern.
> No functional changes intended.
>
> Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

Reviewed-by: Yosry Ahmed <yosry@kernel.org>

---

On Wed, Mar 11, 2026 at 12:52 PM Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> object indices, which describe the location of an object in a zspage,
> cannot be negative. To reflect this most helpers calculate and return
> these values as unsigned ints.
>
> Convert find_alloced_obj, the only function that calculates obj_idx as
> a signed int, to use an unsigned int as well.
>
> No functional change intended.
>
> Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

Reviewed-by: Yosry Ahmed <yosry@kernel.org>

---

[..]
> @@ -1216,6 +1216,11 @@ void zs_obj_write(struct zs_pool *pool, unsigned long handle,
>         class = zspage_class(pool, zspage);
>         off = offset_in_page(class->size * obj_idx);
>
> +       if (objcg) {
> +               WARN_ON_ONCE(!pool->memcg_aware);
> +               zspage->objcgs[obj_idx] = objcg;
> +       }

If pool->memcg_aware is not set the warning will fire, but the
following line will write to uninitialized memory and probably crash.
We should avoid the write if the warning fires.

Maybe:

if (objcg && !WARN_ON_ONCE(!pool->memcg_aware))
       zspage->objcgs[obj_idx] = objcg;

Not pretty, but the same pattern is followed in many places in the kernel.

> +
>         if (!ZsHugePage(zspage))
>                 off += ZS_HANDLE_SIZE;
>

---

> It's a bit awkward that 0 is valid (MEMCG_SWAP). Plus you store these
> values in every pool, even though they're always the same for all
> zswap pools.
>
> How about:
>
> /* zsmalloc.h */
> struct zs_memcg_params {
>         enum memcg_stat_item compressed;
>         enum memcg_stat_item uncompressed;
> };
> struct zs_pool *zs_create_pool(const char *name, struct zs_memcg_params *memcg_params);
>
> /* zswap.c */
> static struct zs_memcg_params zswap_memcg_params = {
>         .compressed = MEMCG_ZSWAP_B,
>         .uncompressed = MEMCG_ZSWAPPED,
> };
>
> then pass &zswap_memcg_params from zswap and NULL from zram.
>
> > @@ -2071,6 +2079,9 @@ struct zs_pool *zs_create_pool(const char *name)
> >       rwlock_init(&pool->lock);
> >       atomic_set(&pool->compaction_in_progress, 0);
> >
> > +     pool->memcg_aware = memcg_aware;
> > +     pool->compressed_stat = compressed_stat;
> > +     pool->uncompressed_stat = uncompressed_stat;
>
>         pool->memcg_params = memcg_params;
>
> And then use if (pool->memcg_params) to gate in zsmalloc.c.

I like this.

I also didn't like the suggested prototype for zs_create_pool(), and
didn't like that compressed_stat and uncompressed_stat did not have
memcg anywhere in the name. Was going to suggest adding a warning if
memcg_aware=false but the stat indices are non-zero, but your
suggestion is so much cleaner.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 03/11] mm/zsmalloc: Introduce conditional memcg awareness to zs_pool
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces conditional memory cgroup (memcg) awareness to the zsmalloc pool, allowing individual pools to be configured as memcg-aware or not. It adds three new fields to the struct zs_pool: memcg_aware, compressed_stat, and uncompressed_stat, which are used to determine whether memory should be allocated for per-compressed object accounting and what statistics to account towards. The patch also updates the zram driver to create a pool that is not memcg-aware. This change aims to minimize hardcoding specific to zswap in zsmalloc by parametrizing the enum indices for compressed and uncompressed stats.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Mar 11, 2026 at 12:51 PM Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> Introduce 3 new fields to struct zs_pool to allow individual zpools to
> be "memcg-aware": memcg_aware, compressed_stat, and uncompressed_stat.
>
> memcg_aware is used in later patches to determine whether memory
> should be allocated to keep track of per-compresed object objgs.
> compressed_stat and uncompressed_stat are enum indices that point into
> memcg (node) stats that zsmalloc will account towards.
>
> In reality, these fields help distinguish between the two users of
> zsmalloc, zswap and zram. The enum indices compressed_stat and
> uncompressed_stat are parametrized to minimize zswap-specific hardcoding
> in zsmalloc.
>
> Suggested-by: Yosry Ahmed <yosry@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

Zswap side LGTM :) And for that:

Acked-by: Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com>

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer pointed out that storing the memcg stats in every pool is inefficient, as these values are always the same for all zswap pools. They suggested introducing a new struct zs_memcg_params to store these values and passing it from zswap.c while passing NULL from zram.c.

Reviewer Johannes Weiner suggested that the patch should be modified to set pool->memcg_params and use it to gate in zsmalloc.c, indicating a need for additional code changes.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Yosry Ahmed liked the patch and appreciated the suggested improvement to zs_create_pool() prototype, which adds memcg awareness to compressed_stat and uncompressed_stat enum indices.
review_comments.raw_body:
I like this.

I also didn't like the suggested prototype for zs_create_pool(), and
didn't like that compressed_stat and uncompressed_stat did not have
memcg anywhere in the name. Was going to suggest adding a warning if
memcg_aware=false but the stat indices are non-zero, but your
suggestion is so much cleaner.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that zram does not support memcg accounting and modified the zs_create_pool call to reflect this, agreeing to a change in v2.

Author agreed to remove the 'memcg_aware' field and instead use bitwise OR of compressed_stat and uncompressed_stat, acknowledging that 0 is not a valid enum state for memcg_stat_item

Author agrees to restructure the code to address Johannes Weiner's feedback, promising to make changes in version 3.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello Johannes,

Thank you for your review! I hope you are doing well : -)

---

Agreed. Originally I thought of removing memcg_aware and doing a bitwise
OR check of the two stats, but thought this was also a bit strange
(also because 0 is not a valid enum state for memcg_stat_item anyways)

---

These definitely look a lot cleaner. Will make these changes in v3!

Thanks again. I hope you have a great day!
Joshua

series_items.subject: [PATCH 04/11] mm/zsmalloc: Introduce objcgs pointer in struct zspage
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces an array of obj_cgroup pointers in the zspage structure to track compressed objects' memory cgroup ownership, if the zs_pool is made memcg-aware at creation time. The error path for alloc_zspage has been simplified by moving it to a jump label. Additionally, the patch includes changes to handle zpdesc allocation and free_zpdesc calls in the error path.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Reviewer suggested using vmalloc instead of kcalloc for allocating the objcgs array, allowing a fallback to vmalloc if necessary
review_comments.raw_body:
I remembered asking this, so my apologies if I missed/forgot your
response - but would vmalloc work here? i.e kvcalloc to fallback to
vmalloc etc.?

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the vmalloc call should be changed to use GFP_KERNEL instead of GFP_HIGHMEM, agreed to make this change in v3.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello Nhat : -)

Thank you for reviewing, and for your acks on the other parts!

You're right, I missed changing that on my end after v1. No reason
vmalloc shouldn't work here, let me make that change in v3.

Thanks, I hope you have a great day!
Joshua

series_items.subject: [PATCH 05/11] mm/zsmalloc: Store obj_cgroup pointer in zspage
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series aims to store the obj_cgroup pointer in zspage, enabling the propagation of object cgroups from the zswap/zram layer down to zsmalloc. The patch modifies several kernel files, including drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c and mm/zsmalloc.c, to handle the lifetime and charging of the obj_cgroup.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential bug where writing to uninitialized memory could occur if pool->memcg_aware is not set. Suggested adding a conditional statement to avoid the write and prevent crashes.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the potential issue and agreed to make changes in the next version. Also mentioned sprinkling WARN_ON_ONCEs in other places.

Hello Yosry,

I hope you are doing well. Thank you for reviewing this series! : -)

> If pool->memcg_aware is not set the warning will fire, but the

series_items.subject: [PATCH 06/11] mm/zsmalloc, zswap: Redirect zswap_entry->objcg to zspage
series_items.patch_summary: This patch redirects the zswap layer to use the obj_cgroup pointer stored in the zspage, eliminating duplicate storage and resulting in a net zero memory footprint change.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the patch's impact on memory usage and suggested further analysis to ensure a net zero footprint change.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 07/11] mm/zsmalloc, zswap: Handle objcg charging and lifetime in zsmalloc
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the zsmalloc layer to handle object cgroup (objcg) charging and lifetime management, specifically for zswap entries. It changes how memory is accounted for in zswap, no longer tracking compressed object size but instead the size of the slot occupied by the entry. This brings memory consumption representation closer to accuracy. Additionally, it removes an unnecessary newline in obj_free and updates related functions.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the obj_cgroup_get() reference is for the pointer, not the charge, and suggested moving it to when adding and storing zspage->objcgs[], to avoid confusion in verifying the series.

The reviewer noted that the patch was misleadingly acquiring a reference to entry->objcg before checking if objcg is NULL, and suggested reordering the code to avoid this issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
Note that the obj_cgroup_get() reference is for the pointer, not the
charge. I think it all comes out right in the end, but it's a bit
confusing to follow and verify through the series.

IOW, it's better move that obj_cgroup_get() when you add and store
zspage->objcgs[]. If zswap stil has a reference at that point in the
series, then it's fine for there to be two separate obj_cgroup_get()
as well, with later patches deleting the zswap one when its
entry->objcg pointer disappears.

---

[ I can see that this was misleading. It was really getting a
  reference for the entry->objcg = objcg a few lines down, hitching a
  ride on that existing `if (objcg)`. ]

> -       mod_memcg_state(memcg, pool->uncompressed_stat, -1);

nit: seems a bit awkward lol?

series_items.subject: [PATCH 09/11] mm/vmstat, memcontrol: Track ZSWAP_B, ZSWAPPED_B per-memcg-lruvec
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series refactors the tracking of ZSWAP_B and ZSWAPPED_B statistics to account for compressed objects split across nodes, improving accuracy at the cost of introducing a small inaccuracy in node stats due to their PAGE_SIZE granularity. The changes decouple updates of node stats and memcg-lruvec stats, allowing for more precise accounting.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the introduction of a small inaccuracy in node stats and suggested exploring alternative solutions to address this issue.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 10/11] mm/zsmalloc: Handle single object charge migration in migrate_zspage
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses memory migration issues in zsmalloc by handling single object charge migration and transferring lruvec and node statistics across affected nodes. It introduces a new approach to uncharge and re-charge compressed objects, simplifying the process for spanning objects that can span multiple pages.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the patch's approach to uncharge-charging compressed objects, suggesting it may not be efficient for all cases. Asked for more information on how this new method handles spanning objects.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses charge migration in zsmalloc by handling zpdesc substitutions, which may change the node of compressed objects and their lruvec association. It introduces a new function zs_migrate_lruvec to transfer per-lruvec and node-level stats when a zspage replaces a zpdesc from a different NUMA node.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/mempolicy: track page allocations per mempolicy

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/mempolicy: track page allocations per mempolicy

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds per-policy page allocation counters to track allocations driven by each memory policy (mempolicy) on a NUMA node. The new counters, exposed in /proc/vmstat and memory.numa_stat, follow the existing numa hit/miss/foreign style and provide detailed information about intentional vs fallback allocations. The counters are tracked per-memcg (memory control group) and per-node, allowing for more accurate investigation of pressure on a NUMA node.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the stats exposed via /proc/vmstat are guarded by CONFIG_NUMA, not CONFIG_MEMCG, and suggested using mod_node_page_state to make global counters work regardless of cgroup configuration.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello JP!

The stats are exposed via /proc/vmstat and are guarded by CONFIG_NUMA, not
CONFIG_MEMCG. Early returning overhere would make it inaccuate. Does
it make sense to use mod_node_page_state if memcg is not available,
so that these global counters work regardless of cgroup configuration.

review_comments.author: JP (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about inconsistent behavior when memcg is disabled, proposed an if-else statement to handle this case and update stats accordingly.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Shakeel Butt expressed concern about the number of new metrics added per-memcg, citing a significant memory cost for each additional metric.
review_comments.raw_body:
I have not looked into what these metrics mean but these are too many, at least
for the memcg. For the memcg, there is significant memory cost for each metric
added to memcg.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Shakeel Butt noted that the new page allocation counters are monotonic increasing metrics, suggesting they are more useful as a rate of change rather than their absolute value, and asked if tracepoints and BPF could be used to achieve this instead.
review_comments.raw_body:
This seems like monotonic increasing metrics and I think you don't care about
their absolute value but rather rate of change. Any reason this can not be
achieved through tracepoints and BPF combination?

review_comments.author: JP (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged Shakeel's concern about the placement of new statistics, explaining they are intended for use alongside existing per-node reclaim stats and would be collected from the same source.
review_comments.raw_body:
We have the per-node reclaim stats (pg{steal,scan,refill}) in
nodeN/vmstat and memory.numa_stat now. The new stats in this patch would
be collected from the same source. They were meant to be used together,
so it seemed like a reasonable location. I think the advantage over
tracepoints is we get the observability on from the start and it would
be simple to extend existing programs that already read stats from the
cgroup dir files.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Shakeel Butt expressed concern that adding 18 counters for per-memcg tracking would be too costly, estimating a single counter's cost at 200KiB to MiBs on typical production machines, and suggested decoupling these stats from the rstat infrastructure to reduce overhead.
review_comments.raw_body:
Convenience is not really justifying the cost of adding 18 counters,
particularly in memcg. We can argue about adding just in system level metrics
but not for memcg.

counter_cost = nr_cpus * nr_nodes * nr_memcg * 16 (struct lruvec_stats_percpu)

On a typical prod machine, we can see 1000s of memcg, 100s of cpus and couple of
numa nodes. So, a single counter's cost can range from 200KiB to MiBs. This does
not seem like a cost we should force everyone to pay.

If you really want these per-memcg and assuming these metrics are updated in
non-performance critical path, we can try to decouple these and other reclaim
related stats from rstat infra. That would at least reduce nr_cpus factor in the
above equation to 1. Though we will need to actually evaluate the performance
for the change before committing to it.

review_comments.author: JP (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: Author is willing to compromise on per-cgroup granularity, suggesting a change from tracking page allocations per mempolicy per-memcg to globally per-node statistics.
review_comments.raw_body:
I could trade off the per-cgroup granularity and change these stats to
become global per-node stats.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Johannes Weiner noted that the patch does not handle the !CONFIG_MEMCG case correctly in mem_cgroup_lruvec() and mod_lruvec_state(), and suggested adding a dummy for mem_cgroup_from_task() to simplify the code.
review_comments.raw_body:
mem_cgroup_lruvec() and mod_lruvec_state() already do the right thing
for !CONFIG_MEMCG. Add a dummy for mem_cgroup_from_task() and you can
do a single, shared sequence for both configs.

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Vlastimil expressed concerns that adding more counters (e.g., for preferred vs preferred_many policies) does not provide a clear benefit and may be redundant compared to tracing, requesting alternative solutions.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think I've been on of the folks on previous versions arguing against the
many counters, and one of the arguments was it they can't tell the full
story anyway (compared to e.g. tracing), but I don't think adding even more
counters is the right solution. Seems like a number of other people
responding to the thread are providing similar feedback.

For example I'm still not sure how it would help me if I knew the
hits/misses were due to a preferred vs preferred_many policy, or interleave
vs weithed interleave?

review_comments.author: JP (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the number and complexity of policy-specific page allocation counters, proposing to simplify the implementation by using a single aggregated policy triplet (hit/miss/foreign) per policy instead of triplets for each policy.
review_comments.raw_body:
How about I change from per-policy hit/miss/foreign triplets to a single
aggregated policy triplet (i.e. just 3 new counters which account for
all policies)? They would follow the same hit/miss/foreign semantics
already proposed (visible in quoted text above). This would still
provide the otherwise missing signal of whether policy-driven
allocations to a node are intentional or fallback.

Note that I am also planning on moving the stats off of the memcg so the
3 new counters will be global per-node in response to similar feedback.
